string.text: {=E17IMA_desc}In 1700, the Imazighen (Berbers), the indigenous people of North Africa, lived in a complex landscape of imperial overlap and local autonomy. While coastal cities and fertile plains in the Regency of Algiers, Tunisia, and Libya were under Ottoman suzerainty, and Morocco was ruled by the newly established Alaouite Dynasty, many Imazighen maintained de facto independence in remote mountain strongholds and desert regions. Societies were organized through tribal structures, clans, and village councils known as the jama’ah, which governed based on customary law and honor codes. This era saw a cultural duality where the Imazighen identity, characterized by the Tamazight language and unique artistic traditions in textiles and pottery, persisted alongside a deep-seated commitment to Sunni Islam. In the south, the Tuareg people controlled vital trans-Saharan trade routes, operating as nomadic Free Men who mediated the flow of goods and ideas between the Mediterranean and Sub-Saharan Africa.
